3A_Bibliographic.citation: Li, X., Zhao, Z., & Yang, G. (2023). Screen media use affects subcortical structures, resting-state functional connectivity, and mental health problems in early adolescence. Brain Sciences, 13(10), 1452.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Curated data from the baseline (T1) and year 2 of follow-up (T2) in the 4.0 release were used in the present study because imaging data were collected every other year. The final sample consisted of 4619 participants (Male = 2396).

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen media use at baseline: passive media (TV/movies + videos), video games, social media (texting + social networking + video chat), total screen time
3C_Exposure.modalities: passive media (TV/movies, online videos), video games, social media (texting, social networking, video chat)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous hours/day (weekday/weekend averaged into 3 categories plus total)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Participants reported how many hours per weekday/weekend day they spend on six types of screen media, including television shows/movies, online videos, video games, texting, social media, and video chat. We further divided screen media use into three categories based on its interactivity and the time spent. Passive media use consists of watching television shows/movies and watching online videos. Social media use is a composite of texting, video chatting, and visiting social networking sites. Video game playing is considered the third type of screen media use.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: CBCL mental health subscales (anxiety/depression, withdrawn, somatic, social, thought, attention, rule-breaking, aggressive, internalizing, externalizing) and subcortical brain volume and resting-state functional connectivity at year 2
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) raw scores; FreeSurfer subcortical volumetrics; Gordon parcellation resting-state functional connectivity (12 networks x 8 subcortical regions = 96 connectivity measures)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Raw scores from the Child Behavior Checklist were used to measure syndromes of anxiety/depression, withdrawn behaviors, somatic complaints, thought problems, attention problems, rule-breaking behavior, aggressive behavior, social problems, internalizing problems, and externalizing problems. We used measures of subcortical volume (n = 9) in our analyses. Using rsfMRI time courses, cortical network to subcortical connectivity was calculated.
3D_Outcome.notes: Multiple outcome domains: mental health and brain. Coded as brain since brain measures are central.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Linear mixed models (LMMs) with research site as random effect; half-longitudinal mediation models for brain-mediation analyses
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: sex, age, race/ethnicity, family income, baseline level of dependent variable, mean framewise displacement and FD-squared (for connectivity analyses)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We used linear mixed models (LMMs) in the R lme4 package to test the association between screen media use and mental health outcomes, as well as brain measures. We covaried for sex, age, race/ethnicity, family income, and the baseline level of dependent variables in all analyses. The research sites were modeled as random effects. We controlled for multiple comparisons using the false discovery rate (pFDR < .05).

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Standardized beta from LMM controlling for baseline rule-breaking
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: more total screen time was associated with increased rule-breaking behavior (b = .044, SE = .012, pFDR = .003)
more passive media use was also associated with increased rule-breaking behavior (b = .038, SE = .012, pFDR = .021)
more video game playing was associated with increased withdrawn/depressed symptoms (b = .041, SE = .013, pFDR = .021)
more social media use was associated with the reduced volume of the hippocampus (b = -.010, SE = .004, pFDR = .039)

3G_Reporting_Quality.large_n_verbatim: In light of the substantial size of the subject cohort examined in this investigation, even effect sizes characterized by extreme diminutiveness attain statistical significance.
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: It is important to note that the observed effect sizes in this study were of modest magnitude, yet they possess the potential for substantive significance. The significance of these seemingly modest effect sizes is underscored by the recognition that their accumulation, when compounded over time and/or across diverse individuals, could yield consequential implications.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper acknowledges small effect sizes and large N, but then argues for their importance through accumulation logic. CIs not reported for primary LMM results.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: By understanding the unique characteristics and impacts associated with specific types of screen media, policymakers can develop targeted strategies that promote healthy screen habits and safeguard the well-being of the younger generation.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.population_override_verbatim: The significance of these seemingly modest effect sizes is underscored by the recognition that their accumulation, when compounded over time and/or across diverse individuals, could yield consequential implications.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion emphasizes that different types of screen media have differentiated associations with mental health and brain outcomes, arguing against treating all screen time as equivalent. It acknowledges the small effect sizes but argues they may accumulate over time, and recommends that policymakers develop targeted strategies. The mediation analysis through brain structure was not significant after FDR correction, and the paper acknowledges the limited number of time points as a constraint.
